share-of-people-who-feel-close-to-at-lea:
Share of people who feel close to at least one person

Share of respondents who said "yes" when asked whether there is a special person they feel very close to.

This horizontal bar chart, dated 2023, ranks countries by the share of respondents who answered "yes" when asked whether there is a special person they feel very close to. Bars are arranged in descending order, with percentage values labeled at the right end of each bar. Nigeria sits at the top with 93%, followed by the Philippines at 90%. Israel records 88%, while Egypt and Spain both register 87%. Sweden and the United States each show 86%. Poland and Germany are tied at 85%, and Hong Kong follows at 84%. Australia and South Africa share 83%, and the United Kingdom, Kenya, and India all record 82%. Brazil and Argentina are tied at 81%, and Mexico sits at 79%. Indonesia and Turkey both show 77%, Tanzania registers 75%, and Japan is at the bottom of the ranking with 67%. Every bar extends well past the midpoint of the chart area, and all values remain above 60%. The span between the highest entry (Nigeria, 93%) and the lowest (Japan, 67%) is 26 percentage points. Several adjacent ranks share identical values, producing visible clusters at 87%, 86%, 85%, 83%, 82%, 81%, and 77%. The gap between Japan and the next-lowest country (Tanzania at 75%) is the widest single step between consecutive bars in the chart.
extent-of-extensive-agriculture:
Extent of extensive agriculture

The extent of extensive agriculture is defined as 'minimal' (less than 1% of land area); 'common' (1 to 20% of land area) or 'widespread' (more than 20%).

This choropleth world map, dated 1850, classifies territories into four categories labeled in the legend: Common (light green), Minimal (pale yellow), None (grey), and Widespread (dark green), plus No data shown by a diagonal-line hatch. South America, including Brazil and surrounding countries, is covered almost entirely by the hatched No-data pattern. Dark green Widespread coverage is concentrated across Western and Central Europe, parts of Eastern Europe, the Levant, India, mainland Southeast Asia, eastern China, scattered territories across Sub-Saharan Africa such as portions of West Africa and the southern cone, Mexico, Central America, several Caribbean islands, and parts of Southeast Asian archipelagos. Light green Common coverage spans the eastern and central United States, stretches across much of Sub-Saharan Africa between the Widespread patches, covers most of Australia and New Zealand, and extends across parts of southern and eastern Asia. Pale yellow Minimal shading dominates the high latitudes of the Northern Hemisphere, including northern Canada, Alaska, Scandinavia, and a broad belt across Russia and northern Asia, and also covers most of North Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the interior of Australia. Grey None patches are visible in the United Kingdom area, Ireland, parts of West Africa and the Sahel, and a few smaller territories. The legend sits along the lower portion of the map above the source credit.
universal-suffrage-men-lexical:
Men's universal right to vote

This does not consider elections not being held, informal restrictions, or legal restrictions based on age, criminal conviction, disability, and local residency.

This choropleth world map, dated 2025, uses three categories defined in the legend: a diagonal-line hatch for No data, orange for No, and light blue for Yes. The overwhelming share of the world's landmass is shaded light blue, covering all of North America except the hatched area, all of South America, all of Europe, all of Russia and Central Asia, all of South and East Asia, Australia, New Zealand, and almost all of Africa. Orange shading, indicating countries where men do not hold a universal right to vote under this classification, is concentrated on the Arabian Peninsula and in the Horn of Africa region. The Arabian Peninsula shows a large orange block covering Saudi Arabia, together with smaller orange territories along its eastern and southern edges. An orange territory is also visible on the east African coast near the Horn. Greenland is the only large territory marked with the diagonal hatch for No data. No legend entry or color band separates countries that partially restrict voting, and no numeric scale is shown; the map is categorical. The legend sits below the map near the center, and the source credit appears in the lower band. No countries in the Americas, Europe, Oceania, or the bulk of the African continent are shaded orange.
annual-growth-of-exports-of-goods-and-se:
Annual growth of exports of goods and services

Exports of goods and services represent the value of all goods and other market services provided to the rest of the world.

This choropleth world map, dated 2023, shows annual growth of exports of goods and services using a diverging color scale. The legend runs from deep red at -10%, through orange and yellow around -5% to -2%, pale tones near 0%, and increasing shades of blue from 2% through 5% to dark blue at 10%. A diagonal-line hatch marks No data. Large hatched regions cover Greenland, most of Russia and its neighbors in northern Asia, and scattered territories elsewhere. In the Americas, Canada and the United States are shaded in light blue bands in the low positive range, while Mexico and several Central American and Caribbean territories sit in orange and red negative bands. Across South America, Argentina and Chile are shaded in strong orange to red negative tones, while Brazil and several neighbors appear in light blue positive bands. Europe shows a patchwork: some central and eastern countries are dark blue in the upper positive range, while several western and southern countries sit in orange or yellow negative bands. In the Middle East and Central Asia, dark blue high-growth shading appears across a cluster of countries including Iran and Mongolia, while nearby territories sit in red and orange. Across Africa, strong red and orange bands appear in parts of the Sahel, Central Africa, and Southern Africa, mixed with blue-shaded neighbors. Australia is shaded in a light-to-mid blue band.
share-who-trust-government:
Share of people who trust their national government

Share of respondents who answered "a lot" or "some" to the question: "How much do you trust your national government?"

This choropleth world map, dated 2020, shows the share of respondents who answered "a lot" or "some" when asked how much they trust their national government. The legend spans 0% to 100% in ten-point bands, running from pale pink at the low end through light lavender and pale blue in the middle to deep navy at the high end; a diagonal-line hatch marks No data. Hatched territories cover sizeable portions of the Sahara and Sahel, parts of the Arabian Peninsula and the Levant, a broad swath of Central Asia, and several smaller regions including Greenland. Dark navy and deep blue shading, in the upper bands of the scale, is concentrated across parts of South and Southeast Asia, a cluster of countries in East Africa, Australia and New Zealand, and a few territories in Northern Europe and Central Asia. North America is shaded in mid-to-dark blue tones. Most of Europe sits in mid-range blue bands, with southern European countries in lighter shades than several northern and central neighbors. Across Latin America, countries are shaded in lighter lavender and pale blue bands in the lower half of the scale, with a mix of mid and low values. Sub-Saharan Africa shows mixed shading, with some countries in dark blue and others in paler bands. The legend and source credit sit along the bottom of the figure.
deaths-from-fires-and-burns:
Deaths from fires and burns

Estimated annual number of deaths due to fire, heat, and hot substances.

This choropleth world map, dated 2023, shows estimated annual deaths from fire, heat, and hot substances. The legend uses a sequential scale with breakpoints at 0, 100, 300, 1,000, 3,000, and 10,000, running from very pale cream at the low end through light orange and mid orange to deep red at the top. A diagonal-line hatch marks No data. India is shaded in the darkest red band at the top of the scale. The United States and Russia appear in a dark red band one step below the darkest. China is also in a dark red band. Across Sub-Saharan Africa, several countries including Ethiopia, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and neighboring territories show mid-to-dark orange shading, and Brazil appears in a mid orange band. Mexico and several South American neighbors sit in lighter orange tones. Most of Western Europe, the Nordic countries, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and a band across North Africa appear in pale cream and light orange tones at the lower end of the scale. Japan sits in a pale cream band. The Arabian Peninsula, the Sahel, and parts of Central Africa are shaded in a mix of light orange and mid orange bands. Hatching marks a small number of territories. The legend sits along the bottom of the map, and the source credit appears below it.
child-mortality-complete-gapminder:
Child mortality

The share of children who died in their first five years of life.

This choropleth world map, dated 2023, shows the share of children who died in their first five years of life. The legend runs from 0% through breakpoints at 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, and 30%, using shades of blue for the lower bands, a transitional pale tone, and orange through red for the higher bands; a diagonal-line hatch marks No data. The highest-band shading, in deep red and orange, is concentrated in a cluster of countries across Sub-Saharan Africa, with the strongest tones in parts of the Sahel, Central Africa, and the Horn of Africa region. Surrounding African countries fall into intermediate orange and pale yellow bands, placing them between 5% and 20%. The rest of the world is colored in dark blue tones corresponding to the lowest bands below 5%, including all of the Americas, all of Europe, Russia, most of Asia, Australia, and New Zealand. A few countries in Central and South Asia sit in slightly lighter blue shades than their neighbors. Greenland is marked with the diagonal-line hatch for No data. The reconstructions are mapped into modern country borders, and the note records that data quality varies strongly across countries and over time. The legend and source credit sit along the bottom of the figure, with the note printed underneath.
